ground_truth.title: 2025 Best Diet Wins Gold for Wellness and Disease Prevention
ground_truth.author: Sandee LaMotte
ground_truth.main_content: The award-winning Mediterranean diet has done it again, capturing 2025's "best of the best" award from U.S. News & World Report, which puts out a yearly list of the most and least beneficial diets as ranked by nutrition professionals. The Mediterranean diet has won top honors since 2019 for its focus on eating fruits, vegetables, grains, olive oil, nuts and seeds, while emphasizing the importance of meals with family and friends and daily exercise. The diet also slashes consumption of sweets and recommends small amounts of dairy and meat, especially red meat. Fish, however, is a staple, especially fatty fish such as sardines. Using the new five-star rating system, the trio of Mediterranean, Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH), and the flexitarian diets each received 4-plus stars as the best overall diets. The DASH diet emphasizes limiting salt intake in its goal to reduce blood pressure, while the flexitarian diet allows an occasional indulgence of meat or poultry in its vegetarian approach. All are plant-based diets that suggest limiting refined, ultraprocessed foods, red meat and added sugars. The 2025 report has also included new rankings for diets designed to help chronic conditions. In the new best diet report, the DASH diet won top ranking (4.9 stars) for both heart health and blood pressure control. Dr. Jesús Luévano Jr., MD, MS, an assistant professor of gastroenterology at Morehouse School of Medicine in Atlanta, said people with digestive issues should not use the U.S. News & World Report rankings to decide on their diet without first having a thorough discussion with their attending physician. "My concern is that people may look at these rankings and then assume that because it's done by nutrition experts, it's the be-all, end-all, and therefore don't have productive conversations with their physicians, who can really help focus on the best foods for them," said Luévano. For irritable bowel syndrome, judges gave the low FODMAP diet 4.8 stars. The diet focuses on reducing foods and carbs that ferment and create extra water in the bowels, which can cause cramping, pain and diarrhea. "Because there are so many subtypes of irritable bowel syndrome, that is probably the best example of where diet really should be individualized for each patient," Luévano said. "Every patient has his or her unique food triggers."
with: The award-winning Mediterranean diet has done it again, capturing 2025's "best of the best" award from U.S. News & World Report.
Using this new system, the trio of Mediterranean, Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension, or DASH, and the flexitarian diets each received 4-plus stars as the best overall diets.

People with digestive issues should not use the U.S. News & World Report rankings to decide on their diet without first having a thorough discussion with their attending physician, said Jesús Luévano Jr., MD, MS, an assistant professor of gastroenterology at Morehouse School of Medicine in Atlanta.

"My concern is that people may look at these rankings and then assume that because it's done by nutrition experts, it's the be-all, end-all, and therefore don't have productive conversations with their physicians, who can really help focus on the best foods for them," said Luévano.
For irritable bowel syndrome, judges gave the low FODMAP diet 4.8 stars. The diet, developed in Australia, focuses on reducing foods and carbs that ferment and create extra water in the bowels.
